title: Chapter 8 Quiz: WAN Technologies
description: Comprehensive quiz covering MPLS, VPNs, cellular technologies, leased lines, Metro Ethernet, remote access, and SD-WAN

questions.question: What is the bandwidth of a T1 leased line?
questions.explanation: T1 provides 1.544 Mbps bandwidth over 24 channels (DS0s) of 64 Kbps each. T3 provides 44.736 Mbps (28 T1s). E1 (European) is 2.048 Mbps. While slow by modern standards, T1 lines still exist in legacy infrastructure. Newer alternatives include fiber, cable, and wireless.

questions.question: Which WAN technology uses labels to forward packets?
questions.explanation: MPLS (Multiprotocol Label Switching) uses short labels instead of IP addresses for forwarding, enabling traffic engineering, QoS, and VPNs. Labels are added at ingress, swapped at transit routers, and removed at egress. MPLS operates between Layer 2 and Layer 3. Common in enterprise WANs but being supplemented by SD-WAN.

questions.question: What VPN protocol operates at Layer 2 and encapsulates PPP frames?
questions.explanation: L2TP (Layer 2 Tunneling Protocol) encapsulates PPP frames for transmission over IP networks. L2TP itself doesn't provide encryption, so it's typically combined with IPsec (L2TP/IPsec). Uses UDP port 1701. Supports multiple protocols. PPTP is legacy and insecure. IPsec operates at Layer 3.

questions.question: A company needs to connect two remote offices over the internet. Which WAN technology creates a secure, encrypted tunnel between the sites?
questions.explanation: A site-to-site VPN creates an encrypted tunnel over the internet between two locations. MPLS is a private WAN service, Metro Ethernet is a metropolitan connection, and ISDN is a legacy dial-up technology.

questions.question: What two main protocols does IPsec use for security?
questions.explanation: IPsec uses AH (Authentication Header) for authentication and integrity, and ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload) for encryption, authentication, and integrity. ESP is more commonly used as it provides confidentiality. Both can be used together. IKE (Internet Key Exchange) establishes security associations.

questions.question: Which VPN type provides encrypted access for individual remote users to the corporate network?
questions.explanation: Client-to-site (remote access) VPN allows individual users to securely connect to the corporate network from anywhere using VPN client software. Site-to-site connects entire networks. Uses IPsec, SSL/TLS, or proprietary protocols. Common for remote workers. Requires authentication (credentials, certificates, MFA).

questions.question: What is the primary advantage of SSL VPN over IPsec VPN?

options.text: Works through web browsers without client software

questions.explanation: SSL/TLS VPN (clientless VPN) works through standard web browsers without special client software, making deployment easier. Uses HTTPS (port 443) which passes through most firewalls. Good for contractors and BYOD. IPsec VPNs require client software but provide better performance and support more protocols. SSL VPN limited to web applications.

questions.question: Which cellular technology generation introduced LTE?
questions.explanation: 4G introduced LTE (Long-Term Evolution) with speeds of 100+ Mbps. 2G provided voice and text. 3G added mobile internet (2-3 Mbps). 5G provides multi-gigabit speeds, ultra-low latency, and massive IoT support. LTE-Advanced improved 4G speeds. 4G LTE revolutionized mobile data.

questions.question: What latency improvement does 5G provide compared to 4G LTE?
questions.explanation: 5G reduces latency to 1-5 milliseconds (ultra-low latency) compared to 30-50 ms for 4G LTE. This enables real-time applications: autonomous vehicles, remote surgery, AR/VR, industrial automation. 5G also provides multi-gigabit speeds (up to 20 Gbps theoretically) and supports millions of devices per square km.

questions.question: Which 5G frequency band provides the highest speeds but shortest range?
questions.explanation: Millimeter wave (mmWave) 5G (24-100 GHz) provides multi-gigabit speeds but has very short range (few hundred meters) and poor building penetration. Mid-band balances speed and coverage. Low-band provides wide coverage but slower speeds. Carriers deploy all three for comprehensive coverage.

questions.question: What is SD-WAN?

options.text: A type of fiber optic cable
Software-defined WAN using multiple transport types with centralized management

questions.explanation: SD-WAN (Software-Defined WAN) uses multiple transport types (MPLS, Internet, LTE, 5G) with centralized cloud-based management and application-aware routing. Benefits: cost savings, better performance, simplified management, automatic failover. Replaces expensive MPLS-only WANs. Uses overlay networks. Major vendors: Cisco Viptela, VMware VeloCloud, Fortinet.

questions.question: Which SD-WAN feature routes traffic based on application requirements and link conditions?
questions.explanation: Application-aware routing identifies applications (DPI) and routes based on requirements and link conditions. VoIP uses low-latency link, bulk data uses cheapest link. Monitors link quality (latency, jitter, packet loss) and steers traffic dynamically. This optimization is SD-WAN's key advantage over traditional static routing.

questions.question: What is Metro Ethernet?

options.text: Carrier-grade Ethernet services across a city

questions.explanation: Metro Ethernet provides carrier-grade Ethernet services across metropolitan areas. Service types: E-Line (point-to-point), E-LAN (multipoint), E-Tree (rooted multipoint). Uses fiber infrastructure. Benefits: high bandwidth (1G-100G+), simple, cost-effective. Common for connecting offices within a city. MEF (Metro Ethernet Forum) defines standards.

questions.question: Which remote access protocol provides encrypted command-line access and uses port 22?
questions.explanation: SSH (Secure Shell) provides encrypted command-line remote access on port 22. Replaces insecure Telnet (port 23). Supports public key authentication, port forwarding, and file transfer (SCP, SFTP). Standard for Linux/Unix administration and network device management. SSH-2 is current version.

questions.question: What protocol does Microsoft Remote Desktop use?
questions.explanation: RDP (Remote Desktop Protocol) provides graphical remote access to Windows systems on port 3389. Supports clipboard sharing, drive redirection, audio, and printing. Version 10+ supports RemoteFX for enhanced graphics. Should be secured with NLA (Network Level Authentication), strong passwords, and VPN. VNC is cross-platform alternative.

questions.question: Which WAN technology uses fixed-size 53-byte cells?
questions.explanation: ATM (Asynchronous Transfer Mode) uses fixed-size 53-byte cells (5-byte header, 48-byte payload) for predictable performance and QoS. Supports voice, video, and data. Legacy technology largely replaced by Ethernet and MPLS. Was popular for backbone networks in 1990s-2000s. Complex and expensive.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a split-tunnel VPN configuration?

options.text: Route only corporate traffic through VPN; internet direct
Split traffic between two VPN servers

questions.explanation: Split-tunnel routes only corporate-destined traffic through VPN; internet traffic goes directly. Reduces VPN load and improves internet performance. Security concern: bypasses corporate security controls for internet traffic. Full-tunnel routes all traffic through VPN for complete security. Split-tunnel common for remote workers accessing cloud services.

questions.question: Which protocol establishes IPsec security associations and exchanges keys?
questions.explanation: IKE (Internet Key Exchange) negotiates IPsec security associations (SAs) and exchanges encryption keys. IKEv2 is current version with improved performance and reliability. Uses UDP port 500 (and 4500 for NAT traversal). Establishes Phase 1 (ISAKMP SA) and Phase 2 (IPsec SA). AH and ESP provide data protection.

questions.question: What is the maximum distance for VDSL2?
questions.explanation: VDSL2 (Very-high-bit-rate DSL) provides up to 100 Mbps but only up to 1 kilometer from DSLAM. Distance affects speed: 100 Mbps at 300m, 50 Mbps at 1km. ADSL reaches 5km but slower. Cable and fiber have replaced DSL in many areas. VDSL2 uses existing phone lines.

questions.question: Which cable internet standard provides up to 10 Gbps downstream?
questions.explanation: DOCSIS 3.1 provides up to 10 Gbps downstream and 1 Gbps upstream over coaxial cable using OFDM. DOCSIS 2.0 maxed at 38 Mbps. DOCSIS 3.0 provides 1 Gbps via channel bonding. DOCSIS 4.0 (in development) targets 10 Gbps symmetrical. Cable internet shares bandwidth among subscribers.

questions.question: What is the primary advantage of MPLS over traditional IP routing?
questions.explanation: MPLS enables sophisticated traffic engineering (steering traffic on specific paths) and guaranteed QoS (prioritizing voice/video). Labels allow different treatment for different traffic types. MPLS VPNs provide secure connectivity. However, MPLS is expensive. SD-WAN provides similar benefits using internet at lower cost.

questions.question: Which WAN connection type provides dedicated bandwidth guaranteed by SLA?
questions.explanation: Leased lines (T1, E1, T3) provide dedicated bandwidth with SLA guarantees (uptime, latency, jitter). Point-to-point circuits with consistent performance. Expensive but reliable. Cable/DSL are shared bandwidth (best effort). Fiber dedicated lines (dark fiber, wavelength services) now more common than T-carrier.

questions.question: What is GRE (Generic Routing Encapsulation) used for?
questions.explanation: GRE tunnels encapsulate various protocols (including routing protocols) over IP networks. Creates point-to-point links. Does NOT provide encryption - often combined with IPsec (GRE over IPsec). Supports multicast unlike IPsec alone. Uses IP protocol 47. Common for site-to-site connectivity and routing protocol transport.

questions.question: Which technology provides direct private connectivity from on-premises to AWS bypassing the internet?
questions.explanation: AWS Direct Connect provides dedicated private connectivity (1-100 Gbps) from on-premises to AWS, bypassing the internet. Lower latency, consistent performance, reduced bandwidth costs. Azure has ExpressRoute, GCP has Cloud Interconnect. More expensive than VPN but better for large data transfers and latency-sensitive applications.

questions.question: What is the purpose of PPPoE?
questions.explanation: PPPoE (PPP over Ethernet) encapsulates PPP frames in Ethernet frames, providing authentication (username/password) for DSL and cable internet connections. Establishes point-to-point connection over Ethernet. ISPs use PPPoE for subscriber authentication and accounting. Discovery phase finds PPPoE server, then establishes session.

questions.question: Which satellite internet technology provides two-way communication?
questions.explanation: VSAT provides two-way satellite communication with small dish antennas. Used in remote locations without terrestrial connectivity. Challenges: high latency (500-700 ms due to distance to geostationary orbit), weather interference, expensive. Starlink (LEO satellites) reduces latency to 20-40 ms. Alternative for rural areas.

questions.question: What is the advantage of using out-of-band management for network devices?

options.text: Access devices even when primary network fails

questions.explanation: Out-of-band management uses separate network/connection (console port, dial-up modem, cellular, dedicated management network) to access devices when primary network fails. Critical for disaster recovery and troubleshooting. In-band management uses the production network. OOB requires additional infrastructure but provides reliability.

questions.question: Which VPN technology is considered obsolete and insecure?
questions.explanation: PPTP (Point-to-Point Tunneling Protocol) is obsolete due to known security vulnerabilities. Uses weak MPPE encryption and MS-CHAPv2 authentication (easily cracked). Should never be used. Replaced by IPsec or SSL/TLS VPNs. Still supported in some legacy systems but blocked by security-conscious organizations.

questions.question: What is the purpose of QoS in WAN connections?

options.text: Prioritize critical traffic (voice/video) over less important traffic

questions.explanation: QoS (Quality of Service) prioritizes time-sensitive traffic (VoIP, video conferencing) over less critical traffic (email, file downloads) on constrained WAN links. Mechanisms: classification, marking (DSCP), queuing, policing, shaping. Prevents latency-sensitive applications from degrading during congestion. Essential for converged networks.

questions.question: Which protocol is used for point-to-point serial connections?
questions.explanation: PPP (Point-to-Point Protocol) is Layer 2 protocol for serial point-to-point links (T1, DSL, dial-up). Provides authentication (PAP, CHAP), error detection, and multi-protocol support. Successor to SLIP. PPP over Ethernet (PPPoE) used for DSL/cable. HDLC is Cisco's default serial encapsulation.

questions.question: Which of the following are capabilities of SD-WAN? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Automatic failover with sub-second link monitoring

questions.explanation: SD-WAN provides automatic failover with link monitoring, application-aware routing, centralized management, and can use multiple links simultaneously. It does NOT require dedicated MPLS—it supports Internet, LTE, MPLS, and other transport types.

questions.question: Which of the following are characteristics of 5G NB-IoT and LTE-M cellular technologies? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Low power consumption with 10-year battery life
Support for millions of devices per square km

questions.explanation: NB-IoT and LTE-M are optimized for massive IoT with low power (10-year battery), deep building penetration, massive device density, and low-cost modules. They are NOT designed for high-speed video streaming—that uses consumer 5G (eMBB).

questions.question: Which of the following are features of DMVPN (Dynamic Multipoint VPN)? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Dynamic spoke-to-spoke tunnel creation without hub
Requires static configuration for each spoke pair
Tunnels created on demand and torn down when idle

questions.explanation: DMVPN enables dynamic spoke-to-spoke tunnels using NHRP for discovery and mGRE tunnels. Tunnels are created on demand and torn down when idle. It does NOT require static configuration for each spoke pair—that's the key advantage over traditional VPN.

questions.question: Which of the following are Metro Ethernet service types defined by the MEF? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: MEF defines E-Line (point-to-point), E-LAN (multipoint, all sites communicate), and E-Tree (rooted multipoint, hub-spoke where spokes can't directly communicate). There is no 'E-Mesh' service type. E-Access is sometimes referenced but the three standard service types are E-Line, E-LAN, and E-Tree.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about MTU (Maximum Transmission Unit) are correct? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Jumbo frames increase MTU to 9000+ bytes
VPN overhead can reduce effective MTU
MTU mismatches never cause connectivity issues
Jumbo frames are commonly used in datacenter/SAN environments

questions.explanation: Standard Ethernet MTU is 1500 bytes, jumbo frames extend to 9000+, VPN overhead reduces effective MTU, and jumbo frames are common in datacenters/SANs. MTU mismatches DO cause connectivity issues (fragmentation, dropped packets, black holes).

questions.question: Link aggregation (LAG) using LACP combines multiple physical links into a single logical link for increased bandwidth and redundancy.
questions.explanation: LAG using LACP combines multiple physical links into one logical link, providing increased bandwidth and redundancy. If one link fails, the connection continues on remaining links. Also called EtherChannel (Cisco) or port channel.

questions.question: Dedicated internet provides best-effort bandwidth with no service level guarantees, while shared internet provides guaranteed bandwidth with SLAs.
questions.explanation: It's the opposite. Dedicated internet provides guaranteed bandwidth with SLAs (uptime, latency, packet loss). Shared internet (broadband, cable) is best-effort with no guarantees and bandwidth shared among subscribers.
references: Lesson 71: Internet Connectivity - Dedicated vs Shared

questions.question: VPN concentrators are dedicated appliances designed to handle thousands of concurrent VPN connections with hardware-accelerated encryption.
questions.explanation: VPN concentrators use dedicated hardware acceleration for encryption/decryption to handle thousands of concurrent connections. Enterprise examples include Cisco ASA, Palo Alto GlobalProtect, and Fortinet FortiGate. Standard firewalls typically support fewer concurrent VPN connections.

questions.question: NAT traversal (NAT-T) encapsulates IPsec ESP packets in UDP port 4500 to allow VPNs to work through NAT devices.
questions.explanation: NAT-T encapsulates ESP in UDP (port 4500) because NAT breaks IPsec by modifying IP headers. UDP encapsulation provides port numbers for NAT translation. It is automatically detected and negotiated, and is critical for remote users behind home routers.

questions.question: WAN deduplication can reduce bandwidth usage by up to 90% by eliminating redundant data segments.
questions.explanation: Deduplication eliminates redundant data by sending only unique segments. Receivers reconstruct from local cache. Can reduce WAN bandwidth by 90%+. Combined with compression, caching, and protocol optimization in WAN optimization solutions like Riverbed, Cisco WAAS, and Silver Peak.

questions.question: Compare SNMPv2c and SNMPv3 from a security perspective. Why did it take the industry so long to adopt SNMPv3, and what are the real-world consequences of continued SNMPv2c usage? Include specific vulnerabilities, protocol differences, and a migration strategy.
questions.explanation: This essay tests understanding of network monitoring protocol security evolution.

rubric.criterion: Accurately describes SNMPv2c security model (community strings, no encryption)
Accurately describes SNMPv3 security model (USM: authentication + encryption)
Explains specific attack scenarios enabled by SNMPv2c vulnerabilities
Analyzes adoption barriers (complexity, device support, inertia)
Proposes practical migration strategy with coexistence period

questions.modelAnswer:
**SNMPv2c Security Model**:
- Authentication: Community strings sent in plaintext (effectively passwords in the clear)
- Encryption: None — all data including community strings visible to packet capture
- Access control: Community string is the only access barrier (read-only vs read-write)
- Vulnerabilities: (1) Community string sniffing → full device read/write access. (2) No message integrity → SNMP SET spoofing can reconfigure devices. (3) Default 'public'/'private' community strings left unchanged on many devices

**SNMPv3 Security Model (USM — User-based Security Model)**:
- Authentication: HMAC-MD5 or HMAC-SHA (username + password hashed, never sent in cleartext)
- Encryption: DES or AES (128/256-bit) for payload privacy
- Three security levels: noAuthNoPriv, authNoPriv, authPriv
- VACM (View-based Access Control Model): granular per-user MIB access control
- Engine IDs prevent replay attacks by tying messages to specific agent-manager pairs

**Real-World Consequences of SNMPv2c**:
- Mirai botnet variants scan for default SNMP community strings to compromise IoT devices
- Network recon: reading routing tables, ARP caches, interface configs via SNMP GET
- Device takeover: SNMP SET with read-write community string can change configs, disable interfaces, modify ACLs
- Compliance violations: PCI DSS, HIPAA, and SOC 2 all require encryption of management traffic

**Why Slow Adoption**:
1. **Complexity**: SNMPv3 requires per-user credentials on every device (vs one community string)
2. **Device support**: Many legacy devices only support SNMPv2c. Budget constraints delay hardware refresh
3. **Tooling**: Early NMS platforms (Cacti, Nagios) had poor SNMPv3 support
4. **Inertia**: 'It works, don't touch it' mentality in operations teams
5. **Performance**: Encryption overhead concerned teams monitoring thousands of devices (largely unfounded on modern hardware)

**Migration Strategy**: (1) Inventory all SNMP-enabled devices. (2) Enable SNMPv3 alongside v2c (coexistence). (3) Migrate monitoring tools to v3. (4) Change v2c community strings to complex values during transition. (5) Disable v2c on each device after v3 verification. (6) Block UDP 161/162 except from NMS subnet via ACLs.

questions.question: Which WAN technology uses label switching to forward packets along predetermined paths, providing traffic engineering capabilities and QoS guarantees?
questions.explanation: MPLS prepends labels to packets at the ingress PE (Provider Edge) router. LSRs (Label Switch Routers) forward based on labels—not IP lookups—along LSPs (Label Switched Paths). This enables traffic engineering (directing traffic along specific paths), fast failover, and QoS differentiation (EXP bits for DSCP mapping).

questions.question: A company needs to connect its branch office to headquarters over the internet with encryption. Which VPN technology creates a persistent, always-on tunnel between the two site firewalls?
questions.explanation: IPsec site-to-site VPN creates a persistent encrypted tunnel between two network devices (firewalls/routers), transparently encrypting all traffic between the two sites. SSL VPN is typically for remote user access. PPTP is deprecated (weak encryption). Split-tunnel is a remote access configuration, not a site-to-site technology.

questions.question: What is the primary advantage of SD-WAN over traditional MPLS for a company with 50 branch offices?

options.text: SD-WAN provides guaranteed SLAs backed by the service provider
SD-WAN can use multiple transport types (MPLS + broadband + LTE) with centralized policy control and typically lower cost
SD-WAN eliminates the need for encryption
SD-WAN supports higher bandwidth than MPLS

questions.explanation: SD-WAN's key advantage is transport independence — it creates an overlay across any underlay (MPLS, broadband, LTE, satellite) and centralizes policy management. For 50 branches, this dramatically reduces cost (MPLS is expensive per-site) while enabling application-aware routing — sending critical apps over MPLS and bulk traffic over broadband. MPLS still provides superior SLAs; SD-WAN complements rather than replaces it.

questions.question: Which THREE of the following are characteristics of DSL (Digital Subscriber Line) broadband? (Select three)

options.text: Uses existing telephone copper pair wiring for data transmission
Bandwidth is shared among all subscribers in the neighborhood
ADSL provides asymmetric speeds — faster download than upload
Performance degrades with distance from the DSLAM (central office)
Requires coaxial cable for the last-mile connection

questions.explanation: DSL uses the existing telephone copper pairs, with ADSL providing asymmetric speeds (faster down than up). Performance decreases with distance from the DSLAM — beyond ~5.5 km, ADSL may not be viable. Unlike cable broadband, DSL bandwidth is NOT shared (dedicated per subscriber). Coaxial cable is used by DOCSIS/cable internet, not DSL.

questions.question: 5G NR (New Radio) can operate in both sub-6 GHz bands for wider coverage and mmWave (24-100 GHz) bands for ultra-high bandwidth with limited range.
questions.explanation: 5G NR operates across three spectrum ranges: low-band (<1 GHz, wide coverage, modest speed), mid-band (1-6 GHz, balance of coverage and speed), and mmWave/high-band (24-100 GHz, extreme bandwidth of 1-10 Gbps but limited to ~300m range requiring dense small cells). Most deployments use mid-band; mmWave is urban/venue-specific.

questions.question: What is the primary disadvantage of geostationary satellite internet (e.g., traditional VSAT) compared to low-earth orbit (LEO) satellite constellations (e.g., Starlink)?

options.text: High latency (~600ms round trip) due to 35,786 km orbit altitude

questions.explanation: Geostationary (GEO) satellites orbit at 35,786 km, creating ~600ms round-trip latency (signal travels ~143,000 km total). This makes real-time applications (VoIP, video conferencing) difficult. LEO satellites (Starlink at ~550 km) achieve 20-40ms latency, comparable to terrestrial broadband, making them suitable for interactive applications.

questions.question: In an MPLS VPN, what is the role of the Route Distinguisher (RD) and how does it differ from the Route Target (RT)?

options.text: RD makes VPNv4 routes unique in the provider's BGP table; RT controls which VRFs import/export routes
RD and RT serve the same purpose and are interchangeable
RD encrypts the MPLS label; RT authenticates the PE router
RD is used only in Layer 2 MPLS VPNs; RT is used only in Layer 3

questions.explanation: The Route Distinguisher (RD) is prepended to IPv4 routes to create unique VPNv4 addresses in BGP — this prevents overlapping private IP ranges from different customers from conflicting. The Route Target (RT) is a BGP extended community that controls route distribution — which VRFs should import/export which routes. Two customers can share routes by matching RTs (extranet VPN).

questions.question: A GRE (Generic Routing Encapsulation) tunnel provides encryption by default, making it a secure alternative to IPsec for site-to-site VPNs.
questions.explanation: GRE provides encapsulation but NO encryption or authentication. GRE tunnels are often combined with IPsec (GRE over IPsec) to get both the flexibility of GRE (which can encapsulate multicast, non-IP protocols) and the security of IPsec encryption. Using GRE alone transmits data in cleartext within the GRE headers.

questions.question: Which THREE capabilities differentiate SD-WAN from a traditional WAN router with VPN? (Select three)

options.text: Application-aware routing that can steer specific apps (e.g., Teams, Salesforce) to preferred links
Zero-touch provisioning (ZTP) — branch devices auto-configure from a central controller
Support for OSPF and BGP routing protocols
Centralized orchestrator with single-pane-of-glass management for all branch sites
Ability to create encrypted tunnels over the internet

questions.explanation: SD-WAN differentiators: (1) Application-aware routing uses DPI to identify apps and route them per policy — traditional routers route by destination IP only. (2) Zero-touch provisioning lets a new branch device bootstrap from the central orchestrator with no on-site engineer. (3) Central orchestrator manages policies, monitoring, and templates for all sites. Traditional routers also support OSPF/BGP and IPsec tunnels — those aren't unique to SD-WAN.

questions.question: A retail company with 120 stores nationwide needs to migrate from MPLS ($800/month per store) to a new WAN architecture. Each store has a POS (point-of-sale) system, inventory management application, and security cameras. Headquarters has an on-premise ERP system and a growing number of SaaS applications (Office 365, Salesforce). Design a cost-effective WAN solution that maintains reliability for POS transactions while reducing monthly costs by at least 40%.
questions.explanation: This design question tests WAN technology selection and migration planning.
questions.scenario: Current state: (1) Each store has a 10 Mbps MPLS circuit ($800/month). (2) All traffic backhauled to HQ data center — including SaaS traffic. (3) POS transactions are sensitive — a store cannot sell without WAN connectivity. (4) Most stores have available cable broadband (100+ Mbps, ~$100/month) and 4G/5G coverage. (5) Budget: save at least $460,000/year on WAN costs. (6) Timeline: 12-month migration, cannot disrupt store operations.

rubric.criterion: SD-WAN architecture with dual transport (broadband primary + cellular backup) and cost analysis showing 40%+ savings
Application-aware policies: POS prioritized, SaaS direct-to-internet (local breakout), cameras deprioritized
Reliability design for POS: cellular failover, connection monitoring, SLA thresholds, local caching strategy
Security: encrypted overlays, ZTNA/SASE for SaaS access, firewall at each store (NGFW or cloud-delivered)
Migration plan: phased rollout (pilot → wave deployment), MPLS/SD-WAN coexistence during transition, rollback procedure

questions.modelAnswer:
**Architecture: SD-WAN with Dual Transport**
- Primary: Business-class cable broadband (100 Mbps) — ~$150/month per store
- Secondary: 4G/5G cellular (unlimited business plan) — ~$75/month per store
- SD-WAN appliance per store: Fortinet FortiGate 40F or Cisco Meraki MX67 — ~$20/month amortized
- **Per-store cost: ~$245/month vs $800 MPLS = 69% savings ($66,600/month = $799,200/year savings)**
- Retain MPLS at HQ and top 10 highest-revenue stores (critical path)

**Application-Aware Policies**:
1. **POS transactions** (TCP 443/8443 to payment processor): Highest priority (DSCP EF), sent over best-performing link (lowest jitter). If broadband jitter >30ms, auto-failover to cellular. Forward Error Correction (FEC) enabled.
2. **ERP/Inventory** (TCP 443 to HQ): High priority, prefers broadband, uses IPsec tunnel to HQ SD-WAN concentrator.
3. **SaaS (O365, Salesforce)**: Local internet breakout via SD-WAN — eliminates HQ backhaul. Cloud-based security (Zscaler/Prisma) inspects this traffic before reaching SaaS.
4. **Security cameras**: Lowest priority, rate-limited to 5 Mbps, broadband only (never cellular to avoid data overages). Can buffer locally during outages.

**POS Reliability**:
- Sub-second failover between broadband and cellular (SD-WAN monitors via BFD)
- SLA thresholds: latency <100ms, jitter <30ms, packet loss <0.1% for POS
- Local transaction caching: POS system queues transactions locally if WAN is down, syncs when restored (store-and-forward). Maximum offline window: 4 hours.
- Cellular SIM from different carrier than broadband provider (path diversity)

**Security Layer**:
- All SD-WAN tunnels encrypted (IPsec AES-256-GCM between sites)
- Cloud-delivered security (SASE): SWG, CASB, DLP for direct internet SaaS access
- ZTNA replaces legacy VPN for store manager remote access
- SD-WAN appliance acts as local NGFW (IPS, app control, web filtering)

**Migration Plan (12 months)**:
- Month 1-2: Pilot — 5 stores in one region. Run SD-WAN parallel to MPLS. Compare performance metrics.
- Month 3-4: Wave 1 — 30 stores. Proven stores cut MPLS. Keep cellular as backup.
- Month 5-8: Wave 2 — 60 more stores (15/month cadence).
- Month 9-11: Wave 3 — remaining 25 stores (complex/remote locations last).
- Month 12: Decommission MPLS circuits (except HQ and top 10 stores).
- Rollback: Each store keeps MPLS active for 30 days post-SD-WAN cutover. If SLAs aren't met, revert to MPLS and troubleshoot.
